title: Data Connection - Push data into a stream - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Stream
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry resource that supports real-time data ingestion, defined by a schema, throughput, and keys. Streams receive records via push-based or pull-based ingestion and support event-based workflows.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for configuring data sources, syncs, and ingestion pipelines that connect external systems to the Foundry platform.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications.

extractedEntities.name: Push-Based Ingestion
extractedEntities.description: An event-driven data ingestion pattern where external systems push records into Foundry streams via REST endpoints, as opposed to Foundry pulling data on a schedule.

extractedEntities.name: Pull-Based Ingestion
extractedEntities.description: The standard data ingestion pattern where Foundry periodically pulls data from source systems and syncs it into datasets or streams.

extractedEntities.name: Stream REST API
extractedEntities.description: A set of REST endpoints exposed by Foundry for push-based record ingestion, requiring a dataset resource identifier, branch name, and authentication token.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: A core Foundry resource that stores structured data in files with schema, branching, and versioning support.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Schema
extractedEntities.description: The structure and type definitions for stream data, which can be auto-generated from JSON samples. Defines columns like sensor_id (String), temperature (Double), created_at (Timestamp).

extractedEntities.name: Stream Throughput
extractedEntities.description: Configuration that determines the data processing rate for a stream and impacts the number of partitions used. Options range from Normal to higher throughput settings.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Keys
extractedEntities.description: Keys configured on a stream to guarantee ordering for unique IDs when using multiple partitions.

extractedEntities.name: Listener
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry mechanism for receiving inbound webhooks from systems that cannot authenticate with Foundry or conform to standard stream schemas.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Authentication Token
extractedEntities.description: A token used to authenticate push-based ingestion requests to Foundry stream REST endpoints.

keyInsights: Foundry streams support both push-based (event-driven) and pull-based (scheduled) ingestion patterns, with push-based using standard REST endpoints.
Push-based ingestion requires three pieces of information: the dataset resource identifier, the branch name, and an authentication token.
Streams are configured with schema (data structure), throughput (processing rate/partitions), and keys (ordering guarantees), with schema auto-generation from JSON samples.
Listeners provide a fallback mechanism for systems that cannot properly authenticate with Foundry or conform to standard stream schemas.
Stream throughput configuration directly determines the number of partitions, which affects parallelism and ordering guarantees.

title: Contour - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Contour
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry application providing a point-and-click user interface for performing data analysis on tables at scale, creating interactive dashboards for guided data exploration.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: A core Foundry resource that stores structured data. Contour can save analysis results as new datasets for use in other Foundry tools.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The semantic layer in Foundry that maps data to real-world concepts. Contour is recommended when data is not mapped in the Ontology or when operating on very large datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Analytical Paths
extractedEntities.description: A Contour pattern for organizing complex analyses into structured sequences of boards that flow in series, starting with a dataset and applying transformations and visualizations.

extractedEntities.name: Contour Expression Language
extractedEntities.description: An expression language available in Contour for performing advanced transformations and aggregations beyond point-and-click capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Parameterized Analysis
extractedEntities.description: A Contour pattern for switching between different views of data and results by parameterizing analyses, enabling flexible interactive exploration.

extractedEntities.name: Large Dataset Analysis
extractedEntities.description: Contour is suited for analytical use cases operating on very large datasets, such as joins on over 100,000 objects or aggregations on over 50,000 rows.

keyInsights: Contour is recommended when data is not mapped in the Ontology or when operating on very large datasets (100K+ objects for joins, 50K+ rows for aggregations).
Contour analysis results can be saved as new datasets for use in other Foundry tools, bridging the gap between ad-hoc analysis and pipeline workflows.
Analyses in Contour flow through boards in series, creating structured analytical paths that can be parameterized for flexible data exploration.
Palantir generally recommends using the Ontology layer whenever possible, positioning Contour as a complement for cases where the Ontology is not appropriate.

title: Foundry Architecture - Palantir Learn

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform with an open architecture design, covering security, access controls, and cloud infrastructure organization.

extractedEntities.name: Palantir Learn
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's educational platform (learn.palantir.com) providing video courses, speedruns, and deep dives for training users on Foundry and related products.

extractedEntities.name: Open Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's guiding architectural principle emphasizing openness, allowing integration with external systems and avoiding vendor lock-in.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Security Model
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's approach to security and access controls within the Foundry platform, governing how data and resources are protected.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Cloud Infrastructure
extractedEntities.description: The organization of Foundry's cloud infrastructure, covering how platform components are deployed and managed in cloud environments.

keyInsights: Foundry's architecture is built on guiding principles of openness, with dedicated focus on security, access controls, and cloud infrastructure organization.
Palantir Learn is transitioning from legacy courses to a new format of Speedruns and Deepdives, indicating a shift toward more hands-on, focused educational content.
Foundry architecture is structured around foundational principles and components that together deliver the complete platform experience.

title: Palantir Foundry connector overview - Microsoft Fabric

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform, integrated with Microsoft Fabric via a dedicated connector for bidirectional data exchange.

extractedEntities.name: Microsoft Fabric
extractedEntities.description: Microsoft's unified analytics platform that includes Data Factory, with a supported connector for Palantir Foundry data integration.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Connector for Microsoft Fabric
extractedEntities.description: A connector supported in Data Factory for Microsoft Fabric that enables Dataflow Gen2 source capabilities with on-premises gateway support.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Client Credentials
extractedEntities.description: An authentication method for the Palantir Foundry connector in Microsoft Fabric, using client credential-based authentication.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Token Authentication
extractedEntities.description: A token-based authentication method for connecting to Palantir Foundry from external platforms like Microsoft Fabric.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry OAuth
extractedEntities.description: An OAuth-based authentication method for the Palantir Foundry connector, enabling secure delegated access from Microsoft Fabric.

keyInsights: Palantir Foundry integrates with Microsoft Fabric Data Factory through a dedicated connector supporting Dataflow Gen2 as a data source.
The connector supports three authentication methods: Foundry Client Credentials, Foundry Token, and Foundry OAuth, reflecting Foundry's flexible security model.
The connector supports on-premises gateway deployments, enabling hybrid cloud scenarios where data resides behind corporate firewalls.

title: Palantir Foundry Is 5-10 Years Ahead of Every Other Data Platform - Data Engineer Things

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The foundational semantic layer of Foundry that governs access, powers applications, enables writebacks, and shapes how data behaves across the system. Described as an 'active' model that influences permissions, interface behavior, and system evolution.

extractedEntities.name: Writeback Layer
extractedEntities.description: A controlled layer in Foundry where user edits are stored separately from source data. Blends source data with user interaction guided by the Ontology, ensuring changes are versioned, governed, and auditable.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's integrated data platform combining data modeling, pipelines, governance, and user-facing applications in a single environment.

extractedEntities.name: Branching
extractedEntities.description: A development pattern in Foundry where users create feature branches to make pipeline changes and materialize datasets in isolation before merging, similar to code version control.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology objects that become the backbone of data applications once defined, representing real-world entities with properties that can include writeback-enabled fields.

extractedEntities.name: Governance-by-Design
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's approach where governance is built into the platform's design rather than layered on after the fact, with policies inheriting through project structure and data lineage.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of Ontology objects that can be configured for writeback, allowing business users to edit values in a governed UI while maintaining version history.

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's built-in capability for tracing data from raw ingestion through transformations to downstream outputs, enabling debugging and governance as an integrated development experience.

extractedEntities.name: Writeback Override Pattern
extractedEntities.description: A real-world use case where source system provides incorrect values that cannot be fixed upstream. Foundry's writeback layer allows business users to edit specific fields in a governed UI, with edits stored separately and downstream logic choosing between original and edited values.

keyInsights: Foundry's Ontology is described as 'active' — it is not just metadata but actively influences permissions enforcement, interface behavior, and system evolution over time.
The writeback layer is a key differentiator: it separates user edits from source data while maintaining versioning, governance, and auditability, guided by the Ontology.
Branching in Foundry mirrors software version control, enabling isolated pipeline changes and dataset materialization before merging into production.
Governance in Foundry is embedded in the platform design rather than bolted on, with policies inheriting through project structure and data lineage.
The Ontology model bridges the gap between data engineering and software design, encouraging structure without rigidity.

title: Data Connection - Syncs - Set up a streaming sync - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Sync
extractedEntities.description: A continuously running sync that pulls data from external streaming platforms into Foundry with low latency, as opposed to batch or incremental syncs that run periodically.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for configuring data sources, syncs, and ingestion pipelines connecting external systems to the platform.

extractedEntities.name: Sync
extractedEntities.description: A task in Data Connection that reads specific data from a source and ingests it into Foundry. Can be batch, incremental, or streaming.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications.

extractedEntities.name: Source Exploration
extractedEntities.description: A Data Connection capability that allows users to explore data available in a source (e.g., Kafka topics) before creating a sync, with previews of sample data.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Partitions
extractedEntities.description: Configurable divisions within a stream that determine throughput capacity. Higher partition counts allow greater throughput, with Normal throughput allowing up to 5 MB/s.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Sync Schema
extractedEntities.description: The data structure definition for a streaming sync, which can be auto-detected from the source or manually configured with field types including string, integer, double, boolean, and timestamp.

extractedEntities.name: Project-Level Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Permission model where dataset access is determined by its location within a Foundry Project, used to govern who can access synced streaming data.

extractedEntities.name: Source-Adjacent Dataset Storage
extractedEntities.description: Recommended practice of saving synced datasets next to their source in a Project so they share the same permissions, simplifying data pipeline security.

keyInsights: Streaming syncs differ from batch/incremental syncs by running continuously for low-latency data ingestion rather than executing periodically.
Normal throughput supports up to 5 MB/s per stream, with higher partition counts enabling greater throughput for demanding workloads.
Palantir recommends storing synced datasets adjacent to their source in a Project to inherit matching permissions, simplifying data pipeline security.
Source exploration allows users to preview available data (e.g., Kafka topics and their contents) before committing to a sync configuration.
Streaming sync schema can be auto-detected from the source or manually configured, supporting field types including string, integer, double, boolean, and timestamp.

title: Core Concepts - Views - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: View
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry resource that behaves like a dataset but holds no data files. Composed of the union of backing datasets when read, supporting automatic updates, folder organization, and primary key deduplication.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: A core Foundry resource that stores structured data in files. Views can use datasets as backing sources and can be used as transform inputs but not outputs.

extractedEntities.name: Backing Dataset
extractedEntities.description: One or more datasets that compose a View through union. All backing datasets for a given View must have the same column names and types.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications.

extractedEntities.name: View Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: A configuration on Views that enables automatic deduplication by guaranteeing that each primary key appears in only one row. Once set, the primary key cannot be modified without creating a new View.

extractedEntities.name: View Deduplication Order
extractedEntities.description: Configuration specifying which row to retain when duplicate primary keys exist. Options include selecting by backing dataset priority order or by a deduplication column (latest timestamp, highest value, etc.).

extractedEntities.name: Zero-Copy Dataset Replication
extractedEntities.description: Using Views to replicate a dataset in a different folder location without incurring additional compute and storage costs.

extractedEntities.name: Automatic Union Updates
extractedEntities.description: A View pattern where a build schedule automatically rebuilds the View whenever backing datasets update, providing always-up-to-date union results without manual transforms.

keyInsights: Views are virtual datasets that hold no data files — they are composed by unioning backing datasets at read time, enabling near-instant builds.
Primary key deduplication on Views guarantees uniqueness without transforms, but the key is immutable once set and requires creating a new View to change.
Views can serve as transform inputs but cannot be transform outputs, and should not be used as incremental inputs since deduplication operates within the transaction range only.
All backing datasets must share the same column names and types (though column order can differ), enforcing schema consistency across unioned sources.
Views enable zero-copy dataset replication across different folder locations without additional compute or storage costs.

title: Data Catalog - Palantir Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Data Catalog
extractedEntities.description: An interactive view within Foundry for discovering and navigating curated data and resources, organized into Collections and Files with tag-based filtering.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications.

extractedEntities.name: Compass
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's file and resource management system that includes the Data Catalog, project navigation, and resource organization capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Collection
extractedEntities.description: A grouping mechanism in the Data Catalog that contains curated files organized by topic, audience, or purpose. Anyone can view collections and descriptions but file access requires sharing permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Data Catalog Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Access control model where users can view all collections and descriptions but only access curated files that are shared with them. Organization administrators manage collection curation permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Data Curation
extractedEntities.description: The practice of curating datasets and resources in the Data Catalog to make the most useful or relevant data discoverable, indicated by a purple checkmark badge throughout Foundry.

keyInsights: The Data Catalog distinguishes between metadata visibility (collections and descriptions are visible to all) and data access (governed by sharing permissions).
Organization administrators control who can add or remove resources from collections, creating a governed curation workflow.
Curated data is indicated by a purple checkmark badge throughout Foundry, providing visual discoverability across the platform.
Tag-based filtering and grouping within collections enables structured navigation of large data catalogs by type, analytics category, or region.

title: Guidance for Industrial Data Fabric with Palantir Foundry on AWS

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's SaaS data platform deployed on AWS infrastructure, capable of integrating industrial data sources like ERP, MES, and data lakes with automated pipelines and low-code tools.

extractedEntities.name: Industrial Data Fabric
extractedEntities.description: An architectural pattern where Foundry integrates data from various industrial sources (ERP, MES, data lakes) to build manufacturing applications supporting everything from shop floor scheduling to global operations centers.

extractedEntities.name: AWS
extractedEntities.description: Amazon Web Services cloud platform on which Foundry can be deployed, using services like Amazon S3, EC2, CloudWatch, KMS, and Auto Scaling.

extractedEntities.name: SAML-Based Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Authentication standard used by Foundry when deployed on AWS, with role-based access control and auditing for accessing services and machines.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry AWS Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Foundry deployment architecture on AWS using Amazon S3 for storage, EC2 instances across three Availability Zones for compute, KMS for encryption, and Auto Scaling for resource management.

extractedEntities.name: Data Encryption at Rest
extractedEntities.description: Security feature where all data at rest in Foundry on AWS is encrypted using AWS Key Management Service (KMS).

extractedEntities.name: Data Encryption in Transit
extractedEntities.description: Security feature where all data in transit in Foundry on AWS is encrypted using SSL.

extractedEntities.name: Manufacturing Operations Center
extractedEntities.description: An industrial use case where Foundry provides real-time visibility, decision-making tools, and operational resolution capabilities from shop floor scheduling to global operations.

keyInsights: Foundry operates as a SaaS product on AWS, leveraging S3 for storage, EC2 across three Availability Zones for high availability, and KMS for encryption.
The industrial data fabric pattern integrates ERP, MES, and data lake sources with automated pipelines and low-code ML model training tools.
Security on AWS includes SSL encryption in transit, KMS encryption at rest, SAML-based authentication, and role-based access with full auditing.
Foundry on AWS uses Auto Scaling and S3 Intelligent-Tiering for cost optimization and performance efficiency.

title: Over 150 new sources are now available in Data Connection - Palantir Developer Community

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for configuring data sources and syncs, now supporting over 150 new source types via Palantir-provided JDBC drivers.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications.

extractedEntities.name: Palantir-Provided JDBC Drivers
extractedEntities.description: JDBC drivers provided by Palantir that enable Data Connection to access 150+ source types including DynamoDB, Dataverse, CosmosDB, and LDAP.

extractedEntities.name: Palantir Developer Community
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's community forum for developers to discuss platform features, share solutions, and get support for integration challenges.

extractedEntities.name: Experimental Source Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: A development lifecycle for new Data Connection source types where connectors are released in an Experimental stage and independently progress through lifecycle stages as usage and support expand.

keyInsights: Data Connection expanded from a curated set of connectors to over 150 new source types using Palantir-provided JDBC drivers, dramatically broadening integration capabilities.
New source types include DynamoDB, Dataverse, CosmosDB, and LDAP, covering NoSQL, Microsoft, and directory service ecosystems.
New connectors follow a staged development lifecycle, starting as Experimental and independently maturing as usage and support grow.
Inline documentation is provided within source configuration, reducing the need for external reference during connector setup.

title: Data Connection - Agents - Agent Worker Configuration Reference - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker
extractedEntities.description: A downloadable program installed within organizational networks that polls Foundry via unidirectional HTTPS connections, executes data sync tasks locally, and pushes results back. Credentials are encrypted with the agent's public key.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for configuring data sources, syncs, and ingestion pipelines connecting external systems to the platform.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications.

extractedEntities.name: Unidirectional HTTPS Polling
extractedEntities.description: The agent worker communication pattern where the agent initiates all connections outbound to Foundry via HTTPS, requiring no inbound network access to organizational networks.

extractedEntities.name: Agent Public Key Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism where credentials for agent workers are encrypted with the agent's public key, ensuring credentials are only decryptable by the intended agent.

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker Memory Configuration
extractedEntities.description: Configuration of JVM heap size (default 1 GB) and host memory (recommended 16 GB minimum) for agent workers, determining performance of locally executed capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker Load Balancing
extractedEntities.description: A mechanism where multiple agents can be assigned to a single source connection with jobs randomly distributed among available agents, supporting a configurable maximum of concurrent syncs (default 16).

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Worker vs Agent Worker
extractedEntities.description: Architecture decision where Foundry worker connections should be preferred over agent workers except for specific file-based syncs filtering large data or micro-batching workflows involving inaccessible on-premise systems.

keyInsights: Agent workers communicate via unidirectional outbound HTTPS connections, requiring no inbound network access — making them suitable for secure on-premise deployments.
Credentials for agent workers are encrypted with the agent's public key, ensuring only the intended agent can decrypt and use them.
Agent workers should only be used for specific file-based syncs filtering large data or micro-batching with inaccessible on-premise systems; Foundry workers are preferred otherwise.
Load balancing across multiple agents is random, with a configurable concurrent sync limit (default 16) enforced across all capabilities and assigned sources.
Memory management involves JVM heap (default 1 GB, must not exceed host memory) and recommended host memory of at least 16 GB.

title: Overview - Model Integration - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Model
extractedEntities.description: An artifact in the Palantir platform that encapsulates machine learning logic. Models can be developed in-platform, imported from external environments, or configured as externally hosted APIs.

extractedEntities.name: Modeling Objectives
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry tool serving as 'mission control' for model management, evaluation, review, release, and deployment. Provides governance, automation (uniform evaluation), and CI/CD for continuous downtime-free deployment.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform supporting data integration, transformation, governance, and operational applications including model integration.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The operational layer of the Palantir platform that connects digital assets to real-world counterparts. Models are registered for use within the Ontology to power interactive workflows.

extractedEntities.name: ML Feedback Loop
extractedEntities.description: A pattern where production data, outcomes, application metrics, and user actions feed back into modeling teams for monitoring, understanding, and improving production model performance.

extractedEntities.name: Model Integration Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: The complete lifecycle for models in Foundry: development or import, integration via Modeling Objectives, deployment, operationalization in the Ontology, and continuous feedback loops.

extractedEntities.name: Model Sources
extractedEntities.description: Models can originate from in-platform development (scikit-learn, TensorFlow, OR-tools), external environments (notebooks, third-party tools, container registries), or externally hosted APIs.

extractedEntities.name: Operational Model Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Using Modeling Objectives to safely deploy models in an operational capacity, enabling users and systems to make decisions backed by high-trust models within the Ontology.

keyInsights: Palantir takes an expansive approach to model integration: models can be developed in-platform, imported from external environments, or configured as externally hosted APIs.
Modeling Objectives provide a unified 'mission control' covering governance, uniform evaluation automation, and CI/CD for continuous downtime-free model deployment.
Once registered in the Ontology, models power operational interactive workflows backed by high-trust guarantees around lineage, security, versioning, reproducibility, and auditing.
ML feedback loops from production data, outcomes, and user actions provide modeling teams with data for monitoring, improving, and adapting models to new circumstances.
Modeling Objectives extend beyond traditional ML Ops to cover simulation and optimization use cases, not just prediction models.

title: Foundry Rules - Core Concepts - Object Model - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Rules
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry capability for managing automated business rules that are applied to data, with support for proposals (audit and review workflows) for rule change management.

extractedEntities.name: Rule
extractedEntities.description: A standard Ontology object in Foundry Rules consisting of metadata properties (name, description, author, type), custom properties (alert severity, assignee, priority), and a logic property containing compressed JSON match conditions.

extractedEntities.name: Proposal
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology object in Foundry Rules representing a submitted change to a rule, analogous to a pull request. Contains the rule ID, proposal metadata (author, timestamp, status, reviewer), and the diff of changes.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The foundational Ontology concept used by Foundry Rules. Both Rules and Proposals are standard objects with properties and Actions.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Actions used in Foundry Rules to create the approval flow for rule proposals, enabling customizable workflows matching operational or regulatory requirements.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes on Rule and Proposal objects including metadata (name, description, author), custom fields (alert_severity, priority), and specialized fields (logic as compressed JSON, diffs for proposals).

extractedEntities.name: Alerting Pattern
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry Rules pattern where rules define alert severity, assignee, and priority properties that are applied to filtered data to generate operational alerts.

extractedEntities.name: Categorization Pattern
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry Rules pattern where rules define group and sub-group properties to categorize data based on match conditions.

extractedEntities.name: Rule Proposal Workflow
extractedEntities.description: A change management pattern analogous to pull requests where rules can have multiple proposals that go through submission, review, and approval/rejection states before changes are applied.

extractedEntities.name: Rule Logic JSON Grammar
extractedEntities.description: Match conditions for rules stored as a compressed JSON blob that conforms to a specific grammar for consistent serialization.

keyInsights: Foundry Rules models both Rules and Proposals as standard Ontology objects, demonstrating how the Ontology serves as a universal modeling layer even for system-level constructs.
Rule logic is stored as a compressed JSON blob conforming to a specific grammar, enabling consistent serialization and programmatic manipulation of match conditions.
The Proposal workflow is analogous to software pull requests, supporting multiple concurrent proposals per rule with full audit trail (author, timestamp, status, reviewer).
Proposals are optional — since Foundry Rules uses standard objects and Actions, the approval workflow can be customized to match any operational or regulatory requirements.
Rules support both alerting patterns (severity, assignee, priority) and categorization patterns (group, sub-group), with custom properties configurable per workflow.

title: AIP Logic - Getting started - Palantir Docs

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry application for building LLM-powered functions using a no-code visual development environment with inputs, blocks, and outputs. Functions can be composed, tested, and released for platform-wide use.

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI Platform that encompasses various AI capabilities including AIP Logic, AIP Assist, and integration with LLMs across the Foundry platform.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: A callable unit in the Ontology that can be created via AIP Logic. Logic functions take typed inputs and produce outputs, with blocks defining the processing chain.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic Block
extractedEntities.description: A processing unit within an AIP Logic function. Types include create variable, apply action, execute function, and use LLM. Block outputs chain into subsequent blocks.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic Input Types
extractedEntities.description: Supported input types for Logic functions: array, boolean, date, double, float, integer, long, media reference, model, object, object list, object set, short, string, struct, and timestamp.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic Debugger
extractedEntities.description: A debugging interface in AIP Logic that shows the LLM's chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting and the steps taken to produce output, enabling inspection and visualization of results.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology objects that can be used as inputs to AIP Logic functions, including single objects, object lists, and object sets.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Actions that can be applied within AIP Logic blocks using the 'apply action' block type.

extractedEntities.name: Automate
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry application that integrates with AIP Logic, accessible from the Logic sidebar, for automating workflows.

extractedEntities.name: LLM Block Composition
extractedEntities.description: A pattern in AIP Logic where multiple LLM and processing blocks are chained together, with outputs from one block feeding into subsequent blocks to create complex AI-powered functions.

keyInsights: AIP Logic provides a no-code visual environment for building LLM-powered functions by composing blocks (create variable, apply action, execute function, use LLM) that chain together.
Logic functions support a wide range of input types including Ontology-native types (object, object list, object set, struct) alongside primitives and media references.
The Debugger shows the LLM's chain-of-thought prompting, enabling developers to inspect and understand the reasoning behind each output.
Logic function outputs can be either primitive values/objects or Ontology edits, bridging AI-generated insights directly into the Ontology's operational layer.
AIP Logic integrates with Automate for workflow automation, enabling LLM-powered functions to be triggered as part of broader operational processes.

title: AIP Assist - Overview - Palantir Docs

extractedEntities.name: AIP Assist
extractedEntities.description: An LLM-powered support tool for navigating and understanding the Palantir platform. Provides real-time assistance using NLP and third-party LLMs trained on Palantir documentation, with multi-language support and context awareness.

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI Platform encompassing AIP Assist, AIP Logic, and other AI capabilities integrated across the Foundry platform.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform. AIP Assist is context-aware of which Foundry application the user is currently in.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Assist Modes
extractedEntities.description: Pre-configured operational modes including default AIP Assist (dynamic routing), Platform Documentation Assist (platform docs only), Developer Assist (Foundry APIs and developer examples), and custom AIP Agents.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Agent
extractedEntities.description: A user-developed, interactive LLM-powered assistant equipped with enterprise-specific information that can be registered with AIP Assist for custom support experiences.

extractedEntities.name: Custom Content Sources
extractedEntities.description: Mechanisms for enhancing AIP Assist with custom knowledge: Notepad documents (recommended) or in-platform Markdown files in documentation-type Code Repositories.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Assist
extractedEntities.description: A specialized AIP Assist mode focused on Foundry APIs and common developer examples, providing targeted assistance for developers building on the platform.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Assist Data Isolation
extractedEntities.description: Security guarantee that AIP Assist does not access user data while providing assistance, fully respecting Palantir's AI Ethics Principles.

keyInsights: AIP Assist is context-aware of the current Foundry application, enabling it to provide more relevant assistance based on the user's current workflow.
Developer Assist mode specializes in Foundry APIs and developer examples, providing a dedicated experience for platform developers distinct from general platform support.
Custom content sources can be added via Notepad documents (recommended) or Markdown files in Code Repositories, enabling enterprise-specific knowledge augmentation.
AIP Assist explicitly does not access user data, maintaining data isolation while still providing contextual assistance — a key trust and security guarantee.
AIP Agents allow organizations to create custom interactive assistants equipped with enterprise-specific information, extending AIP Assist beyond platform documentation.
